title: Cited 23 June 2026: Project Cosmos launch | Science ‘under attack’ at Bonn | Emissions inequality - Carbon Brief
gist: Carbon Brief's research roundup launches Project Cosmos, a 1.8m-paper climate-science database whose Cosmos 500 ranking shows US institutions dominate (nearly half of top authors) just as Trump-era funding cuts threaten them. It also flags coordinated political attacks on climate science at Bonn, a near-strongest-on-record El Niño, and new data quantifying wealth-driven emissions inequality and rising heat-stress exposure.

themes.theme: Project Cosmos database and citation concentration

insights.claim: Carbon Brief launched Project Cosmos, a database of more than 1.8 million academic papers, books and reports on climate change, built around the more than 100,000 references in IPCC reports plus four additional data sources.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: A comprehensive citation map enables identifying knowledge gaps and research opportunities across climate science.
insights.locator: This week, Carbon Brief launched Project Cosmos

insights.claim: The US dominates climate-science citations, with almost half of the 500 most highly-cited authors based at US institutions, while global-south experts account for only 4% of Cosmos 500 authors and women only 10%.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: Heavy US concentration makes the field acutely vulnerable to US funding cuts; China leads among global-south countries.
insights.quote: Almost half of the 500 most highly-cited authors are from US institutions.

insights.claim: At UN climate talks in Bonn, dozens of countries called out coordinated attacks aimed at undermining climate science, with India and Saudi Arabia opposing draft-text calls to encourage scientific work on minimising any overshoot of 1.5C.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: Geopolitical resistance to overshoot-minimisation science signals weakening multilateral consensus on the science base for policy.

insights.claim: US oil-industry allies, including a secretive opposition-research group scouring scientists' emails, are targeting a National Academies extreme-weather attribution report, prompting two of the 15-panel members to resign.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: Pressure campaigns on attribution science could chill research used in climate litigation and liability claims.

insights.claim: The Trump administration reversed its decision to dismantle the Ocean Observatories Initiative, a $368m deep-sea observation system.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: A rare reversal preserves a major ocean-monitoring asset amid broad US science defunding.

insights.claim: NOAA announced that El Niño has officially begun and forecasts suggest the event could be among the strongest ever recorded, while scientists debate whether climate change is intensifying El Niño.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: A near-record El Niño implies elevated commodity, agriculture, and disaster risk in 2026-27 and stress on carbon sinks.

insights.claim: The top 10% of consumers are responsible for $1.7-5.7tn of environmental damage each year, surpassing international climate and biodiversity financing gaps.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: Frames wealthy consumers' footprint as exceeding global finance gaps, supporting arguments for asset/wealth-based climate policy.

insights.claim: Calculating emissions based on asset ownership suggests wealthier people are responsible for an even higher share of global greenhouse gas emissions than past studies indicated.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: Asset-based accounting strengthens the case for taxing capital/wealth as a climate-policy lever.

insights.claim: New research estimates climate change could cause damage equivalent to 10% of UK GDP (range 2-20%) if global warming reaches 4C by 2100.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: Quantifies macro-scale economic exposure to high-warming scenarios for sovereign risk assessment.
insights.locator: Equivalent damage to the UK’s GDP

insights.claim: One billion additional people face at least one day of extreme heat stress (universal thermal climate index above 46C) each year compared to the 1970s, driven by both population growth and rising global temperatures.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: Massive growth in heat-exposed population signals demand for cooling, adaptation infrastructure, and health-system strain.

insights.claim: Sea level rise has quadrupled the frequency of extreme coastal sea-level events since 1900 and nearly tripled since the 1970s the number of days coastal water levels exceed average tide-gauge readings.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: Accelerating coastal-flood frequency raises insurance, real-estate, and infrastructure risk in low-lying areas.

insights.claim: A preprint finds that periods of simultaneous low wind and solar generation, known as renewable energy droughts, will intensify progressively as the planet warms.
insights.speaker: Ayesha Tandon
insights.so_what: Worsening renewable droughts strengthen the investment case for storage, firm capacity, and grid resilience.
